feedback.text: This act has great points and contains practices that I've personally witnessed in video games. On top of this, I would like to bring the attention to the fact that we do not truly own the games we buy. Nowadays, most games require an internet connection to play, regardless of a game being singleplayer only. When the publishers and devs stop supporting their games, so does our ability to continue playing them. When buying a game, it has never been stated or communicated clearly by the publisher that this game will eventually stop receiving support at a specific date, let alone an unknown date. An end-of-life plan for live-service games and other games that require an internet connection would certainly be a huge step forward in Digital Fairness in Video Games. Even when a game eventually loses their official support, releasing an offline mode or letting players host private servers to play with their friends/others, would be a huge improvement and lets us own the games we bought again. On another note, censorship caused by the payment processor monopoly (such as VISA), rings an alarm bell for me. At any given moment could VISA or Mastercard force digital storefronts to stop selling certain games because they do not like/agree with the content of those games (NSFW, violence, etc). It is absurd that a payment processor can dictate what I decide to spend my money on. There is no Digital Fairness in that whatsoever. I would like to see more transparency, security and fairness in the future. This seems very important to me in a digitalised world.
